comments.body: You can not simply print an object using System.out.println. You will have to override toString method. Check stackoverflow.com/questions/29140402/… for reference.

Ohhh I see... Thanks! I imported PiggyBank bank2 as an object and with that it comes with all of the information and such so I was able to do double thisValue = this.totalValue + bank2.getValue() I appreciate it :)

answers.body:
You need to create that combinePiggy(PiggyBank) method and then add the values of the parameter to the corresponding values you call this on.

Somewhat abstract and simplified example to get you started (you should do the real thing yourself to have a learning effect):

class Thing {
  int x;
  int y;

   void combine(Thing otherThing) { 
     x += otherThing.x; 
     y += otherThing.y;
   }
}

comments.body: Thank you I will be sure to watch the video and take your advice.

answers.body:
There is so much wrong with this. First of all, it does not make sense to have those attributes listed as type double. For example: pennies = 0.5; this makes no sense, as you cannot have half a penny in a piggy bank. This needs to be adjusted for all accessor and mutator methods.

public class PiggyBank {
    
      int pennies, nickels, dimes, quarters;
      double bankTotal;
      .
      .
      .
      public int getP()
  {
      return pennies;
  }
  public int getN()
  {
      return nickels;
  }

  public int getD()
  {
      return dimes;
  }

  public int getQ()
  {
      return quarters;
  }
}

Additionally, the combinePiggy method should not have a return type, as you are not returning anything. Furthermore, as the main method suggests, the combinePiggy method should have another piggybank as a parameter. The method should then add the number of each piggy bank together, as well as the total of each bank.

public void combinePiggy(PiggyBank bank2)
  {
      pennies += bank2.pennies;
      nickels += bank2.nickels;
      dimes += bank2.dimes;
      quarters += bank2.quarters;
      bankTotal += bank2.bankTotal;
  }

Another thing, judging by the main method, your class needs a toString method. A toString method is exactly what the name implies. It converts an object into a string. (I'll leave it for you to write the appropriate toString to fit your main method)

  public String toString(){
    return "This piggy bank has " + pennies + " pennies, " + nickels + " nickels, " + 
    dimes + " dimes, " + quarters + " quarters.";
  }

Also, you cannot just add the number of coins in order to get a bank total. Example: You have a penny, a nickel, a dime and a quarter. 0.01+0.05+0.10+0.25=0.41. The way you are doing it, you're bank total would just add up the number of coins.

  public PiggyBank(int p, int n, int d, int q)
  {
      pennies = p;
      nickels = n;
      dimes = d;
      quarters = q;
      bankTotal = pennies*0.01 + nickels*0.05 + dimes*0.1 + quarters*0.25;
  }

I think I've done more than answer your question. I will leave the addPenny as well as the setter methods to you. It seems you are very new to Java. I suggest you watch a few videos and read some documentation about the language before you tackle this assignment. Here is a video that more or less sums up the language.

Record

In modern Java, when the purpose of your class is to transparently communicate shallowly-immutable data, define the class as a record.

record PiggyBank( int p , int n , int d , int q ) {}

Let objects add themselves

To that we need to add a couple of methods.

totalCents

One method totals up the value of all the currency as a count of cents. No need to make the total a member field, just calculate on the fly.

Notice that for that total, we must return a 64-bit long rather than an 32-bit int. Otherwise, putting very large int numbers of coins into a very large piggy bank could result in integer overflow.

plus

The other method we need does the work of adding the current piggy bank object with another piggy bank object. Rather than alter (“mutate”) either, we create a third piggy bank object that combines the value of the other two.

This code belongs on the PiggyBank class as the work is simple with no need for orchestration with other objects.

record PiggyBank( int p , int n , int d , int q )
{
    PiggyBank plus ( final PiggyBank that )
    {
        return
                new PiggyBank(
                        this.p + that.p ,
                        this.n + that.n ,
                        this.d + that.d ,
                        this.q + that.q
                );
    }

    long totalCents ( )
    {
        return
                this.p
                        + ( this.n * 5 )
                        + ( this.d * 10 )
                        + ( this.q * 25 );
    }
}

Try that code with your sample data.

PiggyBank alice = new PiggyBank( 5 , 5 , 5 , 5 );
PiggyBank bob = new PiggyBank( 2 , 3 , 4 , 1 );
PiggyBank combined = alice.plus( bob );

Dump to console:

IO.println( "alice = " + alice + " | total = " + alice.totalCents() );
IO.println( "bob = " + bob + " | total = " + bob.totalCents() );
IO.println( "combined = " + combined + " | total = " + combined.totalCents() );

When run:

alice = PiggyBank[p=5, n=5, d=5, q=5] | total = 205
bob = PiggyBank[p=2, n=3, d=4, q=1] | total = 82
combined = PiggyBank[p=7, n=8, d=9, q=6] | total = 287

In real work, we would perform data validation. This would include adding an explicit constructors to our record, forbidding negative number of coins. And we would verify that we are not passed a null piggy bank in the plus method.

I would only keep the number of each coin as int variables, and calculate the total value on demand with method getTotalValue:

...
    private static final double PENNY_VALUE = 0.01;
    private static final double NICKEL_VALUE = 0.05;
    private static final double DIME_VALUE = 0.10;
    private static final double QUARTER_VALUE = 0.25;

    private int pennies;
    private int nickels;
    private int dimes;
    private int quarters;

    public PiggyBank(int p, int n, int d, int q) {
        pennies = p;
        nickels = n;
        dimes = d;
        quarters = q;
    }
...

    public double getTotalValue() {
        return pennies*PENNY_VALUE
                + nickels*NICKEL_VALUE
                + dimes*DIME_VALUE
                + quarters*QUARTER_VALUE;
    }

To be able to print the contents of a piggy bank as required you will need to override the toString method:

@Override
public String toString() {
    return String.format("The piggy bank currently contains:%n"
            + "%d pennies, %d nickels, %d  dimes, %d quarters%n"
            + "for a total value of: %.2f",
            pennies,  nickels, dimes, quarters, getTotalValue());
}

As you can see in the example, when you call one.combinePiggy(two), one holds the total, and two is reset to zero. That can be done as follows:

public void reset() {
    pennies = nickels = dimes = quarters = 0;
}

...

public void combinePiggy(PiggyBank other) {
    pennies += other.pennies;
    nickels += other.nickels;
    dimes += other.dimes;
    quarters += other.quarters;
    other.reset();
}

Here's the complete class:

package PiggyChak;

public class PiggyBank {
    private static final double PENNY_VALUE = 0.01;
    private static final double NICKEL_VALUE = 0.05;
    private static final double DIME_VALUE = 0.10;
    private static final double QUARTER_VALUE = 0.25;

    private int pennies;
    private int nickels;
    private int dimes;
    private int quarters;

    public PiggyBank(int p, int n, int d, int q) {
        pennies = p;
        nickels = n;
        dimes = d;
        quarters = q;
    }

    // Getters and setters omitted for brevity

    public void reset() {
        pennies = nickels = dimes = quarters = 0;
    }

    public double getTotalValue() {
        return pennies*PENNY_VALUE
                + nickels*NICKEL_VALUE
                + dimes*DIME_VALUE
                + quarters*QUARTER_VALUE;
    }

    public void combinePiggy(PiggyBank other) {
        pennies += other.pennies;
        nickels += other.nickels;
        dimes += other.dimes;
        quarters += other.quarters;
        other.reset();
    }

    @Override
    public String toString() {
        return String.format("The piggy bank currently contains:%n"
                + "%d pennies, %d nickels, %d  dimes, %d quarters%n"
                + "for a total value of: %.2f",
                pennies,  nickels, dimes, quarters, getTotalValue());
    }
}

title: Combining two objects in Java
body:
I'm new to java and I'm confused as to how I can combine these two objects. Sorry if I am not clear / if this has been asked before.

I need to add one.PiggyBank to two.PiggyBank

We are not allowed to change the program used to output results

public class PiggyBank {
    double pennies;
    double nickels;
    double dimes;
    double quarters;
    double totalValue;
    double bankTotal;

    public PiggyBank(int p, int n, int d, int q)
    {
        pennies = p;
        nickels = n;
        dimes = d;
        quarters = q;
        totalValue = pennies + nickels + dimes + quarters;
    }

    // Getters and setters omitted for brevity


    public double combinePiggy(double bank2)
    {
        // how to implement combinePiggy?
        // bankTotal = bank1 + bank2;
        return 0d;
    }

    public static void main(String[] args) {
        PiggyBank one = new PiggyBank(5, 5, 5, 5);
        PiggyBank two = new PiggyBank(2, 3, 4, 1);

        System.out.println("Account 1: " + one + "\n");
        System.out.println("Account 2: " + two + "\n");

        one.combinePiggy(two);

        System.out.println("Account 1: " + one + "\n");
        System.out.println("Account 2: " + two + "\n");
    }
}

How to implement combinePiggy?
